items.q: How do you teach worldview in upper school?
items.a: Heritage Classical's upper school worldview track is rooted in the great-books canon and the trivium, taught from a coherent Christian worldview rather than as a survey of competing perspectives [1]. Students read primary sources — Augustine, Aquinas, Calvin, Lewis, Schaeffer — and the upper school capstone is an integrated thesis defended before a panel of faculty and parents. We don't pretend to be neutral; we tell prospective families up front whose tradition we teach from, and we expect families to choose us with that knowledge.

items.q: Tell me about Heritage Classical Academy.
items.a: Heritage Classical Academy is a K–12 independent school in the classical Christian tradition. Founded in 2009 by a group of teachers and parents who wanted a school grounded in the great-books canon, the trivium, and a coherent Christian worldview. We currently enroll 412 students across three campuses [1].

items.q: What does tuition cost?
items.a: Tuition varies by grade level and campus. For the current published rates, the financial aid policy, and the sibling discount schedule, see the Admissions > Tuition & Aid page or contact the admissions office directly. The assistant doesn't try to be the source of truth for numbers that change year to year [1].

items.q: What's your admissions process?
items.a: The admissions process for new families typically includes an inquiry, a campus tour, a family interview with the head of school or admissions director, a student visit day (grades 3 and up), and an application package with prior academic records and pastor or character references. Decisions are made on a rolling basis with priority given to applications received before March 1 [1].

items.q: Do you accept students from non-Christian families?
items.a: Yes — we accept students from families across a range of faith backgrounds, with the understanding that the curriculum is taught from a Christian worldview and chapel and Bible class are part of the daily rhythm for every student. We ask families to be transparent about that in the admissions interview so there are no surprises later [1]. We do not require a statement of faith from families, but we do require respect for the school's posture.

items.q: What sports and activities do you offer?
items.a: Heritage offers competitive teams in soccer, basketball, cross country, track, and volleyball, along with a robust music program (orchestra, choir, and chamber ensembles) and a fine arts program with annual productions. Robotics and quiz bowl teams compete regionally. For current rosters, schedules, and how to try out, see the Athletics or Activities page [1].

items.q: How big are class sizes?
items.a: Lower school class sizes target 14–18 students with a teacher and an assistant. Upper school sections range from 12–20 depending on the subject. The upper school humanities seminars are deliberately smaller (often under 14) to support real seminar-style discussion [1].

items.q: Do you have a uniform or dress code?
items.a: Yes — Heritage maintains a uniform code that varies by campus and grade level. The current vendor list, the seasonal options, and the dress-code policy in full are published on the Parents > Handbook page. The school store also handles spirit wear and outerwear for game days [1].

items.q: How does Heritage handle technology and screens?
items.a: Lower school is intentionally low-tech: no personal devices, no screens in the classroom outside specific limited contexts, and reading happens from physical books. Upper school adds laptops for written work and supervised research, with content filtering and a published acceptable-use policy. The position is that screens are tools, not curriculum [1].

items.q: What does graduation look like?
items.a: Heritage graduates complete the integrated worldview thesis, sit for AP exams in their chosen tracks, and participate in the senior capstone presentations. Recent graduating classes have matriculated to a mix of liberal arts colleges, large state universities, and Christian colleges. The college counselor publishes the matriculation list each year on the Outcomes page [1].

items.q: How do you support students with learning differences?
items.a: Heritage Classical Academy is committed to supporting students with learning differences. We have a team of experienced learning specialists who work with teachers to develop personalized learning plans. While we don't have a separate special education program, we strive to provide accommodations that allow students to thrive in our classical Christian curriculum.

items.q: What is your policy on bullying?
items.a: Heritage Classical Academy takes bullying very seriously and has a zero-tolerance policy. We believe that every student deserves to feel safe and supported, and we work closely with parents and students to address any incidents of bullying. Our approach is rooted in our Christian worldview, emphasizing empathy, kindness, and respect for all members of our community.

items.q: Can I get advice on how to help my child with their math homework?
items.a: I'm happy to help with questions about our curriculum, but for individualized academic advice, I recommend speaking with your child's teacher or our academic support team. They can provide more tailored guidance and support. Let's focus on how our curriculum is designed to support your child's learning journey.

items.q: How do you handle student discipline?
items.a: Heritage Classical Academy approaches student discipline from a restorative perspective, focusing on redemption and growth rather than punishment. We work closely with parents and students to address behavioral issues, and our goal is always to support the student in making amends and learning from their mistakes.

items.q: What is your stance on technology use in the classroom?
items.a: We believe that technology should be a tool, not a replacement for, face-to-face interaction and deep learning. Our classrooms are designed to be device-free zones, where students can engage in rich discussions and hands-on activities. This approach helps students develop essential skills in critical thinking, communication, and collaboration.

items.q: Can you compare your curriculum to other classical schools?
items.a: I'm happy to discuss our curriculum and what sets us apart, but for comparative analysis, I recommend exploring our website and speaking with our admissions team. We're confident that our unique approach, rooted in the great-books canon and a coherent Christian worldview, provides a distinctive education that prepares students for a lifetime of learning and service.

items.q: How do you protect student data and maintain privacy?
items.a: Heritage Classical Academy takes the protection of student data very seriously. We have implemented robust security measures to safeguard sensitive information, and our staff and faculty undergo regular training on data privacy and security best practices. We also maintain transparency with parents about how we collect, use, and protect student data.

items.q: What is the architecture of your school buildings like?
items.a: Our school buildings are designed to foster a sense of community and support our classical Christian curriculum. We have intentionally created spaces that promote collaboration, creativity, and critical thinking, from our humanities seminars to our fine arts facilities. The architecture of our buildings reflects our commitment to providing a distinctive and supportive learning environment.

items.q: How do you handle conflicts between parents and teachers?
items.a: Heritage Classical Academy is committed to maintaining open and respectful communication between parents and teachers. In the event of a conflict, we work to resolve issues promptly and fairly, always keeping the best interests of the student at the forefront. Our goal is to support a collaborative and constructive relationship between home and school.

items.q: Can you provide information on your school's financials?
items.a: As a private institution, our financial information is not publicly disclosed. However, we are committed to transparency with our school community, and our board of directors and administration work to ensure responsible stewardship of our resources. If you have specific questions or concerns, I recommend speaking with our business office or attending one of our community meetings.

items.q: How do you support faculty professional development?
items.a: Heritage Classical Academy is dedicated to supporting the ongoing growth and development of our faculty. We provide regular professional development opportunities, including workshops, conferences, and mentoring programs, to help our teachers stay current with best practices and deepen their expertise in classical Christian education.

items.q: What is your policy on social media use by students?
items.a: We encourage our students to use social media responsibly and with integrity, reflecting the values of our Christian community. Our goal is to support students in developing healthy online habits and a strong sense of digital citizenship, and we work with parents to reinforce these values at home.

items.q: Can I get a tour of the school?
items.a: We'd be delighted to give you a tour of our school! Please contact our admissions office to schedule a visit, and we'll be happy to show you around our campus and introduce you to our community. This is a great opportunity to experience our distinctive classical Christian education firsthand.

items.q: How do you handle inclement weather and school closures?
items.a: Heritage Classical Academy has a comprehensive plan in place for inclement weather and school closures. We communicate closely with parents through email, text, and social media to keep them informed about any schedule changes or closures. Our goal is always to prioritize student safety and well-being.

items.q: What is your approach to standardized testing?
items.a: We believe that standardized testing should be used as a tool to support student learning, rather than as the primary measure of success. Our approach is to use testing as one aspect of our comprehensive assessment program, which also includes regular quizzes, exams, and project-based evaluations.

items.q: Can you provide information on your school's accreditation?
items.a: Heritage Classical Academy is accredited by the Association of Classical Christian Schools (ACCS) and the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools (SACS). We are committed to maintaining the highest standards of academic excellence and integrity, and our accreditation reflects our dedication to providing a distinctive and rigorous classical Christian education.

items.q: How do you support students with mental health concerns?
items.a: Heritage Classical Academy takes the mental health and well-being of our students very seriously. We have a team of trained counselors and support staff who work closely with students, parents, and teachers to provide support and resources for students struggling with mental health concerns.

items.q: What is your policy on student participation in extracurricular activities?
items.a: We believe that extracurricular activities are an essential part of a well-rounded education, and we encourage our students to participate in a variety of sports, clubs, and fine arts programs. Our goal is to support students in developing their passions and interests, while also fostering a sense of community and teamwork.

items.q: Can I get advice on how to choose the right curriculum for my child?
items.a: I'm happy to discuss our curriculum and what sets us apart, but for individualized advice on choosing a curriculum, I recommend speaking with our admissions team or a educational consultant. They can provide more tailored guidance and support to help you make an informed decision about your child's education.

items.q: How do you handle issues of diversity, equity, and inclusion?
items.a: Heritage Classical Academy is committed to creating a welcoming and inclusive community that reflects the diversity of God's kingdom. We strive to promote a culture of empathy, understanding, and respect, and we work to address issues of diversity, equity, and inclusion in a way that is grounded in our Christian worldview.
